chapters.title: How a stubborn old man held a nation together
chapters.text:
World War II left a wound on the human story that has not fully closed. The losses and the crimes of Nazi Germany are still studied, and so is the question of how to lead a country that stands alone against a brutal enemy. Winston Churchill, the British Prime Minister through the worst of it, becomes our guide here. He was a strange choice. He once almost walked naked into a meeting with the American president, and his daily life was a stream of odd habits. But hard times have a way of producing unusual leaders, and the British public chose him anyway.

Churchill was ready for the work. He never hid the size of the threat, and he had a gift for turning fear into grit. The people around him, at home and abroad, started to believe that something better was possible, and that belief became a weapon in its own right.

It is worth remembering how close the world came to losing this fight.

Without Churchill, it is not clear that the United States would have entered the war when it did, and it is not clear that the Soviet Union could have held on without American supplies. The man who watched German bombers from his London rooftop with the glee of a small child may be the single biggest reason the Allies held together. Erik Larson tells Churchill's story through the people around him, his family and his colleagues, and through the daily events of the war. That angle gives the book its pull, because we see the great man through the eyes of the people who had to live with him.

chapters.title: A new captain takes the wheel
chapters.text:
May 10, 1940. The evening air in London carried the faint smell of roses, and a pair of swans drifted on the pond in St. James Park. Far from that quiet scene, the German army was swallowing country after country. Holland fell, Belgium fell, Luxembourg fell. France was the last big power still in the way, and the outlook for Britain was grim. For Winston Churchill, the First Lord of the Admiralty, the moment felt like the chance he had been waiting for.

The British government was fragile. Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain was seventy one and visibly aging. Churchill had spent years attacking him for signing the 1938 Munich Agreement, the deal that handed part of Czechoslovakia to Hitler in the name of peace. The age gap between the two men was small, but the public trusted Churchill, even though the rest of the government did not.

John Colville, a private secretary at Number Ten, considered Churchill careless, nosy, and far too fond of his drinks. In the House of Commons that kind of reputation was a serious problem. A wartime leader was supposed to look like a wartime leader, and the man who liked to work in the bath did not fit the picture. Still, it was Chamberlain himself who recommended Churchill as his replacement, and the choice was made.

Churchill was the kind of gamble a country makes when the safe bets have run out.

Once he was in the seat, Churchill moved fast. He chose his own cabinet, even in the middle of a political mess and a financial one. He made his old friend Max Aitken the Minister of Aircraft Production, and Aitken got to work on the one thing Britain could not do without, which was planes. The successful evacuation from Dunkirk gave the country a flash of hope, and the first German raid on British soil landed on June 5. Churchill used his voice to steady the country, and even Hermann Goring, the head of the German air force, found himself grudgingly impressed. Words, however, were not enough when Paris was about to fall, and the German war machine kept rolling forward.

chapters.title: Britain alone in the summer of 1940
chapters.text:
June 22, 1940. France gave up, despite Churchill's last-minute efforts to keep it in the fight. Britain now stood alone against an enemy that had just crushed most of continental Europe. The French fleet became the next worry. If Germany took control of those ships, its air and naval power could sit right on Britain's doorstep. On top of that, the British government had kept quiet about the sinking of the Lancastria, a troopship that was hit while carrying more than four thousand soldiers. The cover-up fed a wave of public anger at the leadership in London.

In Berlin, Hitler and his inner circle felt sure the war was almost over. Britain had no good reason to keep fighting, in their view, and the Führer even pulled forty divisions out of service after France fell, which was about a quarter of the German army. He had no plan to invade the British Isles, because he knew his navy could not carry it. The only way to break Britain was from the air, and so the German air force began to grow.

The British response was to bet on science and on production. Churchill brought in a physicist known as The Prof, Frederick Lindemann, as his science adviser. Lindemann backed a young colleague, R.V. Jones, who was convinced that German bombers used a double beam at night, and that the signal could be tracked. The trick worked, and British pilots could now find raiders more often. Aitken, at the Ministry of Aircraft Production, pushed the monthly output of aircraft from forty five planes to more than a thousand. By July 1940, the small early raids on British cities were costing Germany three aircrew for every British one lost.

Churchill also made the hard call to attack what was left of the French fleet. It was a brutal decision, but he could not trust Hitler not to use the same ships against Britain. For Hitler, the strike on French ships was the last straw, and he took it as a flat refusal to negotiate. Berlin kept receiving bad intelligence about the state of the Royal Air Force, with reports that painted the British as weak and nearly finished. Goring pushed the same line, even as his own pilots were losing the fight in the sky.

Underestimating a nation's will to live is a dangerous habit.

chapters.title: A quiet month that almost broke the peace
chapters.text:
August 13, 1940. Goring ordered the German air force to attack Britain, and he put on a show of confidence. More than a thousand bombers were lined up, despite the poor weather. Many of the units did not coordinate well, and several of them ran into the RAF and never came home. For the record, the slow German Stuka was a poor match for the British Hurricane fighter.

Britain could not afford to be smug. The damage from German raids was a warning that a landing on the southern coast was still a real risk. London and the rest of the country kept going about daily life. The journalist Virginia Cowles wrote about watching an air battle from her picnic blanket. John Colville, Churchill's private secretary, fell hard for Audrey Paget over the summer, even though her mother held a strange mix of views that included anti-semitic ideas. Colville did not seem to mind.

That kind of prejudice showed the reach of German propaganda. On August 24, the German air force accidentally bombed central London. Hitler had not given the order, and he had planned to wait until the air force was stronger. The mistake was blamed on a new and complicated German navigation device, and the result was a bomb on a neighborhood that Berlin had planned to leave alone. Churchill used the moment to order a British raid on Berlin, and the next night the air raid sirens woke up the German capital for the first time in the war. Goebbels played down the damage. Hitler, looking for a way to prove himself to the German public, ordered the air force to hit London in return.

The British fleet was still in a bad way. Churchill tried to borrow American ships, but Roosevelt was cautious in front of his own people. The deal came together when Britain agreed to lease bases in Newfoundland and Bermuda to the United States. On September 2, eight worn out American destroyers arrived. They were old and barely fit for service, but they were something.

For Britain in August 1940, having enough ships mattered more than having good ones.

chapters.title: Long nights and the strange new shape of the city
chapters.text:
September 7, 1940. Goring climbed to a viewing point on top of Cap Blanc-Nez to watch his bombers attack London. The KGr 100 unit, with 348 planes, crossed the British coast at 4:43 in the afternoon. The RAF was not expecting a raid on the city center, and the second German wave hit at 6:10. British fighters were nearly blind once the sun went down, and that weakness was a real problem. Aitken decided to spread the factories and aircraft storage across the country, so that a single raid could not wipe out the whole production base. The bombs crept closer to the royal family and to Churchill himself. On September 9, a German bomb landed inside the grounds of Buckingham Palace, and later in the month another fell near Chequers, the country house used by the Prime Minister.

The raids killed more than four hundred civilians, and many more were injured. After a while, the air raids became a strange kind of routine. Power cuts were common, people sheltered in the tube stations, and sleep was hard to come by. Food was rationed, and basic items like tampons and toilet paper were hard to find, which made the public health situation worse. The brick dust from bombed buildings got into cuts and caused infections, and that drove an unexpected rise in intimacy in the shelters. Long raids and the feeling that life could end at any moment changed the way people behaved. British morale stayed surprisingly high, and Churchill was a big reason. His speeches gave people a reason to keep going.

In the same month he had to talk to the French people, after a failed British attack on the port of Dakar had been turned back by the pro-German Vichy French. On October 21, 1940, Churchill gave one of his most famous speeches, addressed to the people of France, asking them not to give in to Hitler. At the same time, Hitler was afraid that Churchill was collecting allies around the world. The BBC was making Germans nervous about their own army, and Hitler had to act.

Wars often start because leaders are afraid of losing their grip on power.

The 1939 non-aggression pact with the Soviet Union had never felt solid. As Britain and America grew closer, Hitler worried that the Soviets would join them. He started to plan for a new war, this time against the USSR.

chapters.title: Bombs, germs, and a long winter
chapters.text:
November 5, 1940. Franklin D. Roosevelt won his second presidential election, and the timing was good for Britain. With a new term behind him, he could talk about the war more openly. Roosevelt shared Churchill's view that American safety depended on Britain's ability to defend itself. Lending arms, without sending soldiers, was a way to help the fight that most Americans could accept. The plan became the Lend-Lease Act, and the law tipped the balance in favor of the future alliance against Hitler. In the meantime, Britain was short on military supplies and on basic goods, and the latest German air raids made the mood even darker.

On November 14, 1940, a bright moon lit up the night, and German bombers hit the city of Coventry. The raid exposed a failure of British intelligence, which had not seen it coming. Shelters, a hospital, and a church were hit, and the damage was enormous. The German propaganda machine called the strikes a fair blow at a military target, but the truth was that eleven straight hours of bombing killed 568 people and wrecked more than 2,000 buildings.

Aitken and the King came to Coventry, and the survivors were not kind. Aitken tried to copy Churchill's way of handling a crowd, and it did not work. The civilians blamed Herbert Morrison, the Minister of Home Security, for the poor protection of the city. Even in London, where people were more used to it, the raids left families without power, gas, or water, and the public shelters were dirty and poorly lit.

Disease killed people who had survived the bombs.

The German reconnaissance flights were also showing that Britain was not on the verge of giving up, and that fact was getting on Joseph Goebbels' nerves. Hitler needed peace with Churchill so that he could turn his army east. His plan for the invasion of the Soviet Union, called Case Barbarossa, was already on the drawing board. The year 1941 was about to get much worse.

chapters.title: How a strange friendship helped win a war
chapters.text:
January 10, 1941. A thin, sickly American named Harry Hopkins walked into 10 Downing Street. He was fifty years old and he did not look like a powerful man, but he would soon become the single most important figure in the British-American alliance. Churchill was a generous host during Hopkins's month-long visit, and Hopkins toured the bomb sites in person. He wrote urgent letters back to Roosevelt, and he and Churchill became close friends, often leaning on each other for support during the long months of the war.

Hopkins summed up his own devotion in a line that Churchill liked to repeat. Wherever you go, I will go, he said, and wherever you live, I will live. Your people will be my people. That kind of personal loyalty was rare in politics, and it mattered more than any treaty.

Churchill also had good news from the battlefields. British forces had pushed the Italians and Germans out of parts of the Middle East, Greece, and Norway. The Norwegian campaign was a particular win, sinking fifteen thousand tons of German shipping and capturing a key Enigma machine. Even with all that, the Prime Minister kept expecting a German invasion of Britain. The air raids on London were so heavy that his military advisors began to believe a large landing was coming, which is exactly what Hitler wanted them to think.

In Berlin, the talk was all about invading the Soviet Union. The German army was already building up supplies. For that, peace with Britain was needed, but the air raids on London were not bringing the result Hitler wanted. Several of his own deputies warned him against a two-front war, even quoting back to him from his own book Mein Kampf. The Führer did not listen. On December 18, 1940, he signed the order for Case Barbarossa, the invasion of the Soviet Union. The bombing of Britain kept going, and the RAF struggled to fight back at night, even with the occasional tactical win.

Hitler also expected the United States to come into the war, and he wanted to keep America busy in the Pacific. On March 5, he issued a directive to push Japan into action in the Far East, hoping to draw American attention to that part of the world.

The fear of losing grip pushes leaders to take bigger and bigger risks.

Back in Washington, the Lend-Lease Bill sat in the Senate for a long time. Americans were split, and only about fifty eight percent of the public supported the idea. The number kept slipping. On March 11, the Senate finally passed the bill, and the war took a new turn.

chapters.title: The wheel of history begins to turn
chapters.text:
April 6, 1941. Germany invaded Yugoslavia and attacked Greece. A week earlier, German forces had pushed the British out of Benghazi, a city in Libya. The bombing of Belgrade killed seventeen thousand people and left the city in ruins. As Hitler's plan to move east became clear, Britain and the United States felt both relieved and more worried. The natural wealth of Ukraine and Iraq was at risk of falling into Nazi hands.

Churchill had not asked for American troops, but the spring of 1941 made it obvious that he would need them. The air raids on British cities had not let up. On April 16, London suffered one of the deadliest raids of the war so far. More than a thousand people died, and many more were hurt. Famous buildings were hit, including Christie's auction house, the Admiralty, and St. Peter's Church. Coventry, Birmingham, and Bristol also took serious damage.

Roosevelt slowly grew bolder. He expanded the American naval security zone so that US ships could spot German vessels closer to Britain. Even so, the British military kept losing in the Mediterranean. On April 24 and 25, British troops had to leave Greece, the third major evacuation since Churchill had taken office. The public mood grew sour, and Churchill's hold on power looked weaker by the day. America was not eager to fight for the Middle East unless there was money in it, and that frustrated Churchill. A passionate speech in the House of Commons in May 1941, during a Vote of Confidence, saved his job. Even Goebbels in Berlin admitted, privately, that Churchill had pulled off a political win, even if the speech was full of excuses.

At the same time, the enemy camp was taking losses of its own. In early April, British bombers hit the State Opera House in central Berlin. Hitler hit back with more air raids, including the big raid on April 16.

Then, suddenly, the air attacks stopped at the end of spring. In June, Hitler invaded the Soviet Union, and Britain finally had room to breathe. On December 7, 1941, the news reached the world that Japan had hit the American naval base at Pearl Harbor. America was in the war, and the next four years would be the bloodiest the world had ever seen.

Sometimes not knowing what is coming makes it easier to keep going.

chapters.title: A long year, and the man who led it
chapters.text:
Spring 1940. The air in Britain smelled of flowers and turned earth, and the sound of distant bombs drifted in. The House of Commons was uneasy when Churchill took over as Prime Minister, and many members of Parliament thought the choice was a bad one. The country was on the edge of invasion, and Churchill had to win over a room full of doubters.

Soon after, the French gave up, and Britain stood face to face with the Third Reich, the force that had just rolled over most of Europe. The following year showed that Churchill's rough edges were exactly what the moment needed. He pushed aircraft production to new heights, he won the trust of Roosevelt and of the American public, and he gave the British people the steady voice they needed during a year of bombing. His speeches calmed nerves after each raid. What set him apart from the other leaders of his day was his refusal to break, at home or abroad. Churchill held a frightened country together through relentless attack because he had the kind of leadership that the moment demanded. Even his enemies had to admit he was good at the job. Churchill will always be a controversial figure, but it is still not clear that Britain, or the rest of the world, would have won the war without him.

Try this. Watch the documentary Walking with Destiny on YouTube, or try the History Hit podcast episodes on Churchill's darkest hour and on the Battle of Britain. The story only gets more interesting the closer you look.
